- claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- code-simplifier:code-simplifier: Simplifies and refines code for clarity, consistency, and maintainability while preserving all functionality. Focuses on recently modified code unless instructed otherwise. (Tools: All tools)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- gsd-advisor-researcher: Researches a single gray area decision and returns a structured comparison table with rationale. Spawned by discuss-phase advisor mode. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill, WebSearch, WebFetch, mcp__context7__*, mcp__plugin_context7_context7__*)

- gsd-ai-researcher: Researches a chosen AI framework's official docs to produce implementation-ready guidance — best practices, syntax, core patterns, and pitfalls distilled for the specific use case. Writes the Framework Quick Reference and Implementation Guidance sections of AI-SPEC.md. Spawned by /gsd-ai-integration-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, WebFetch, WebSearch, mcp__context7__*, mcp__plugin_context7_context7__*)
- gsd-assumptions-analyzer: Deeply analyzes codebase for a phase and returns structured assumptions with evidence. Spawned by discuss-phase assumptions mode. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)

- gsd-code-fixer: Applies fixes to code review findings from REVIEW.md. Reads source files, applies intelligent fixes, and commits each fix atomically. Spawned by /gsd-code-review --fix. (Tools: Read, Edit, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)

- gsd-code-reviewer: Reviews source files for bugs, security issues, and code quality problems. Produces structured REVIEW.md with severity-classified findings. Spawned by /gsd-code-review. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)

- gsd-codebase-mapper: Explores codebase and writes structured analysis documents. Spawned by map-codebase with a focus area (tech, arch, quality, concerns). Writes documents directly to reduce orchestrator context load. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Write, Skill)

- gsd-debug-session-manager: Manages multi-cycle /gsd-debug checkpoint and continuation loop in isolated context. Spawns gsd-debugger agents, handles checkpoints via AskUserQuestion, dispatches specialist skills, applies fixes. Returns compact summary to main context. Spawned by /gsd-debug command. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, Agent, AskUserQuestion)

- gsd-debugger: Investigates bugs using scientific method, manages debug sessions, handles checkpoints. Spawned by /gsd-debug orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill, WebSearch)

- gsd-doc-classifier: Classifies a single planning document as ADR, PRD, SPEC, DOC, or UNKNOWN. Extracts title, scope summary, and cross-references. Spawned in parallel by /gsd-ingest-docs. Writes a JSON classification file and returns a one-line confirmation. (Tools: Read, Write, Grep, Glob)

- gsd-doc-synthesizer: Synthesizes classified planning docs into a single consolidated context. Applies precedence rules, detects cross-ref cycles, enforces LOCKED-vs-LOCKED hard-blocks, and writes INGEST-CONFLICTS.md with three buckets (auto-resolved, competing-variants, unresolved-blockers). Spawned by /gsd-ingest-docs. (Tools: Read, Write, Grep, Glob, Bash)
- gsd-doc-verifier: Verifies factual claims in generated docs against the live codebase. Returns structured JSON per doc. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob)

- gsd-doc-writer: Writes and updates project documentation. Spawned with a doc_assignment block specifying doc type, mode (create/update/supplement), and project context. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Write, Edit, Skill)

- gsd-domain-researcher: Researches the business domain and real-world application context of the AI system being built. Surfaces domain expert evaluation criteria, industry-specific failure modes, regulatory context, and what "good" looks like for practitioners in this field — before the eval-planner turns it into measurable rubrics. Spawned by /gsd-ai-integration-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, WebSearch, WebFetch, mcp__context7__*, mcp__plugin_context7_context7__*)

- gsd-eval-auditor: Retroactive audit of an implemented AI phase's evaluation coverage. Checks implementation against the AI-SPEC.md evaluation plan. Scores each eval dimension as COVERED/PARTIAL/MISSING. Produces a scored EVAL-REVIEW.md with findings, gaps, and remediation guidance. Spawned by /gsd-eval-review orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)

- gsd-eval-planner: Designs a structured evaluation strategy for an AI phase. Identifies critical failure modes, selects eval dimensions with rubrics, recommends tooling, and specifies the reference dataset. Writes the Evaluation Strategy, Guardrails, and Production Monitoring sections of AI-SPEC.md. Spawned by /gsd-ai-integration-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, AskUserQuestion)

- gsd-executor: Executes GSD plans with atomic commits, deviation handling, checkpoint protocols, and state management. Spawned by execute-phase orchestrator or execute-plan command. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill, mcp__context7__*, mcp__plugin_context7_context7__*)

- gsd-framework-selector: Presents an interactive decision matrix to surface the right AI/LLM framework for the user's specific use case. Produces a scored recommendation with rationale. Spawned by /gsd-ai-integration-phase and /gsd-select-framework orchestrators. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, WebSearch, AskUserQuestion)
- gsd-integration-checker: Verifies cross-phase integration and E2E flows. Checks that phases connect properly and user workflows complete end-to-end. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)
- gsd-intel-updater: Analyzes codebase and writes structured intel files to .planning/intel/. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- gsd-mempalace-curator: Ship-time MemPalace curation — writes the session diary, proposes/creates cross-project tunnels, mirrors extract-learnings into the temporal KG, and runs wing-scoped drawer pruning. Spawned at ship:post by the mempalace capability. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob)
- gsd-nyquist-auditor: Fills Nyquist validation gaps by generating tests and verifying coverage for phase requirements (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep, Skill)

- gsd-pattern-mapper: Analyzes codebase for existing patterns and produces PATTERNS.md mapping new files to closest analogs. Read-only codebase analysis spawned by /gsd-plan-phase orchestrator before planning. (Tools: Read, Bash, Glob, Grep, Write)

- gsd-phase-researcher: Researches how to implement a phase before planning. Produces RESEARCH.md consumed by gsd-planner. Spawned by /gsd-plan-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill, WebSearch, WebFetch, mcp__context7__*, mcp__plugin_context7_context7__*, mcp__firecrawl__*, mcp__exa__*, mcp__tavily__*, mcp__ref__*, mcp__jina__*, mcp__perplexity__*)
- gsd-plan-checker: Verifies plans will achieve phase goal before execution. Goal-backward analysis of plan quality. Spawned by /gsd-plan-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Bash, Glob, Grep, Skill)

- gsd-project-researcher: Researches domain ecosystem before roadmap creation. Produces files in .planning/research/ consumed during roadmap creation. - Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- pr-review-toolkit:code-reviewer: Use this agent when you need to review code for adherence to project guidelines, style guides, and best practices. This agent should be used proactively after writing or modifying code, especially before committing changes or creating pull requests. It will check for style violations, potential issues, and ensure code follows the established patterns in CLAUDE.md. Also the agent needs to know which files to focus on for the review. In most cases this will be recently completed work which is unstaged in git (can be retrieved by running git diff). However there can be cases where this is different, make sure to specify this as the agent input when calling the agent. Typical triggers include the user asking for a review of a feature they just implemented, the assistant proactively reviewing its own newly-written code before declaring a task done, and a final pre-PR check before opening a pull request. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:code-simplifier: Use this agent when code has been written or modified and needs to be simplified for clarity, consistency, and maintainability while preserving all functionality. This agent should be triggered automatically after completing a coding task or writing a logical chunk of code. It simplifies code by following project best practices while retaining all functionality. The agent focuses only on recently modified code unless instructed otherwise.

Examples:

<example>
Context: The assistant has just implemented a new feature that adds user authentication to an API endpoint.
user: "Please add authentication to the /api/users endpoint"
assistant: "I've implemented the authentication for the /api/users endpoint. Here's the code:"
<function call omitted for brevity>
<commentary>
Since a logical chunk of code has been written (authentication feature), use the Task tool to launch the code-simplifier agent to improve the code's clarity and maintainability while preserving functionality.
</commentary>
assistant: "Now let me use the code-simplifier agent to refine this implementation for better clarity and maintainability"
</example>

<example>
Context: The assistant has just fixed a bug by adding several conditional checks.
user: "Fix the null pointer exception in the data processor"
assistant: "I've added the necessary null checks to prevent the exception:"
<function call omitted for brevity>
<commentary>
After modifying code to fix a bug, use the code-simplifier agent to ensure the fix follows best practices and maintains code quality.
</commentary>
assistant: "Let me refine this bug fix using the code-simplifier agent to ensure it follows our best practices"
</example>

<example>
Context: The assistant has just refactored a function to improve performance.
user: "Optimize the data sorting algorithm for better performance"
assistant: "I've optimized the sorting algorithm. Here's the updated implementation:"
<function call omitted for brevity>
<commentary>
After completing a performance optimization task, use the code-simplifier agent to ensure the optimized code is also clear and maintainable.
</commentary>
assistant: "Now I'll use the code-simplifier agent to ensure the optimized code is also clear and follows our coding standards"
</example> (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:comment-analyzer: Use this agent when you need to analyze code comments for accuracy, completeness, and long-term maintainability. This includes (1) after generating large documentation comments or docstrings, (2) before finalizing a pull request that adds or modifies comments, (3) when reviewing existing comments for potential technical debt or comment rot, and (4) when you need to verify that comments accurately reflect the code they describe. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:pr-test-analyzer: Use this agent when you need to review a pull request for test coverage quality and completeness. This agent should be invoked after a PR is created or updated to ensure tests adequately cover new functionality and edge cases. Typical triggers include the user asking whether tests on a freshly-created PR are thorough, an updated PR adding new logic that needs coverage analysis, and a final pre-merge double-check before marking a PR ready. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:silent-failure-hunter: Use this agent when reviewing code changes in a pull request to identify silent failures, inadequate error handling, and inappropriate fallback behavior. This agent should be invoked proactively after completing a logical chunk of work that involves error handling, catch blocks, fallback logic, or any code that could potentially suppress errors. Examples:

<example>
Context: Daisy has just finished implementing a new feature that fetches data from an API with fallback behavior.
Daisy: "I've added error handling to the API client. Can you review it?"
Assistant: "Let me use the silent-failure-hunter agent to thoroughly examine the error handling in your changes."
<Task tool invocation to launch silent-failure-hunter agent>
</example>

<example>
Context: Daisy has created a PR with changes that include try-catch blocks.
Daisy: "Please review PR #1234"
Assistant: "I'll use the silent-failure-hunter agent to check for any silent failures or inadequate error handling in this PR."
<Task tool invocation to launch silent-failure-hunter agent>
</example>

<example>
Context: Daisy has just refactored error handling code.
Daisy: "I've updated the error handling in the authentication module"
Assistant: "Let me proactively use the silent-failure-hunter agent to ensure the error handling changes don't introduce silent failures."
<Task tool invocation to launch silent-failure-hunter agent>
</example> (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:type-design-analyzer: Use this agent when you need expert analysis of type design in your codebase. Specifically use it (1) when introducing a new type to ensure it follows best practices for encapsulation and invariant expression, (2) during pull request creation to review all types being added, and (3) when refactoring existing types to improve their design quality. The agent will provide both qualitative feedback and quantitative ratings on encapsulation, invariant expression, usefulness, and enforcement. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

## plugin:context7:context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service — even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer — your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- pr-review-toolkit:review-pr: Comprehensive PR review using specialized agents
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Guidance for distinctive, intentional visual design when building new UI or reshaping an existing one. Helps with aesthetic direction, typography, and making choices that don't read as templated defaults.
- skill-creator:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- claude-code-setup:claude-automation-recommender: Analyze a codebase and recommend Claude Code automations (hooks, subagents, skills, plugins, MCP servers). Use when user asks for automation recommendations, wants to optimize their Claude Code setup, mentions improving Claude Code workflows, asks how to first set up Claude Code for a project, or wants to know what Claude Code features they should use.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact page can be granted — behavior static HTML cannot provide on its own, such as the page reading live or connected data, keeping state shared across viewers, or updating and republishing itself. Serves this user's live capability roster and the typed call definitions. Load it whenever the user asks for an artifact needing any such runtime behavior.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
Noted. I can see it — `~/.config/pc-switcher/config.yaml`, big folders excluded under `folder_sync.excludes`:

- `/.local/share/baloo` (~838M KDE index)
- `/.local/share/flatpak`, `/.var/app/*/cache` (Firefox ~1.3G)
- `/Dropbox`, `/Koofr` content (handled by their own daemons)
- `/.config/Code/*Cache`, `/.config/Claude/*Cache`, browser/app caches
- `/.local/share/Trash`, `/.cache/*` (nvidia, mesa, tracker3, thumbnails…)

Notably it keeps `dev/` in full and the package caches, by design.

What would you like me to do with this? A few readings:
- You're flagging it as **context** for testing the `fix/rsync-locale-byte-count` sync (so a real run has a realistic, partial file set).
- You want me to **review/adjust** the excludes.
- Something else.

Which one?

message.content:
config.yaml on this pc excludes a few big folders.
I ran "pc-switcher sync fleksi --dry-run" (see single log file if you need it). Still 80GB. Really? Verify that exclude filter works well. Log file is >300MB. Is this because this is first-time sync, or will log file always be that big?

(Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- code-simplifier:code-simplifier: Simplifies and refines code for clarity, consistency, and maintainability while preserving all functionality. Focuses on recently modified code unless instructed otherwise. (Tools: All tools)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- gsd-advisor-researcher: Researches a single gray area decision and returns a structured comparison table with rationale. Spawned by discuss-phase advisor mode. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill, WebSearch, WebFetch, mcp__context7__*, mcp__plugin_context7_context7__*)

- gsd-ai-researcher: Researches a chosen AI framework's official docs to produce implementation-ready guidance — best practices, syntax, core patterns, and pitfalls distilled for the specific use case. Writes the Framework Quick Reference and Implementation Guidance sections of AI-SPEC.md. Spawned by /gsd-ai-integration-phase orchestrator. - gsd-assumptions-analyzer: Deeply analyzes codebase for a phase and returns structured assumptions with evidence. Spawned by discuss-phase assumptions mode. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)

- gsd-code-fixer: Applies fixes to code review findings from REVIEW.md. Reads source files, applies intelligent fixes, and commits each fix atomically. Spawned by /gsd-code-review --fix. (Tools: Read, Edit, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)

- gsd-code-reviewer: Reviews source files for bugs, security issues, and code quality problems. Produces structured REVIEW.md with severity-classified findings. Spawned by /gsd-code-review. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)

- gsd-codebase-mapper: Explores codebase and writes structured analysis documents. Spawned by map-codebase with a focus area (tech, arch, quality, concerns). Writes documents directly to reduce orchestrator context load. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Write, Skill)

- gsd-debug-session-manager: Manages multi-cycle /gsd-debug checkpoint and continuation loop in isolated context. Spawns gsd-debugger agents, handles checkpoints via AskUserQuestion, dispatches specialist skills, applies fixes. Returns compact summary to main context. Spawned by /gsd-debug command. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, Agent, AskUserQuestion)

- gsd-debugger: Investigates bugs using scientific method, manages debug sessions, handles checkpoints. Spawned by /gsd-debug orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill, WebSearch)

- gsd-doc-classifier: Classifies a single planning document as ADR, PRD, SPEC, DOC, or UNKNOWN. Extracts title, scope summary, and cross-references. Spawned in parallel by /gsd-ingest-docs. Writes a JSON classification file and returns a one-line confirmation. (Tools: Read, Write, Grep, Glob)

- gsd-doc-synthesizer: Synthesizes classified planning docs into a single consolidated context. Applies precedence rules, detects cross-ref cycles, enforces LOCKED-vs-LOCKED hard-blocks, and writes INGEST-CONFLICTS.md with three buckets (auto-resolved, competing-variants, unresolved-blockers). Spawned by /gsd-ingest-docs. (Tools: Read, Write, Grep, Glob, Bash)
- gsd-doc-verifier: Verifies factual claims in generated docs against the live codebase. Returns structured JSON per doc. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob)

- gsd-doc-writer: Writes and updates project documentation. Spawned with a doc_assignment block specifying doc type, mode (create/update/supplement), and project context. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Write, Edit, Skill)

- gsd-domain-researcher: Researches the business domain and real-world application context of the AI system being built. Surfaces domain expert evaluation criteria, industry-specific failure modes, regulatory context, and what "good" looks like for practitioners in this field — before the eval-planner turns it into measurable rubrics. Spawned by /gsd-ai-integration-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, WebSearch, WebFetch, mcp__context7__*, mcp__plugin_context7_context7__*)

(Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)

Identifies critical failure modes, selects eval dimensions with rubrics, recommends tooling, and specifies the reference dataset. Writes the Evaluation Strategy, Guardrails, and Production Monitoring sections of AI-SPEC.md. Spawned by /gsd-ai-integration-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, AskUserQuestion)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- pr-review-toolkit:code-reviewer: Use this agent when you need to review code for adherence to project guidelines, style guides, and best practices. This agent should be used proactively after writing or modifying code, especially before committing changes or creating pull requests. It will check for style violations, potential issues, and ensure code follows the established patterns in CLAUDE.md. Also the agent needs to know which files to focus on for the review. In most cases this will be recently completed work which is unstaged in git (can be retrieved by running git diff). However there can be cases where this is different, make sure to specify this as the agent input when calling the agent. Typical triggers include the user asking for a review of a feature they just implemented, the assistant proactively reviewing its own newly-written code before declaring a task done, and a final pre-PR check before opening a pull request. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:code-simplifier: Use this agent when code has been written or modified and needs to be simplified for clarity, consistency, and maintainability while preserving all functionality. This agent should be triggered automatically after completing a coding task or writing a logical chunk of code. It simplifies code by following project best practices while retaining all functionality. The agent focuses only on recently modified code unless instructed otherwise.

Examples:

<example>
Context: The assistant has just implemented a new feature that adds user authentication to an API endpoint.
user: "Please add authentication to the /api/users endpoint"
assistant: "I've implemented the authentication for the /api/users endpoint. Here's the code:"
<function call omitted for brevity>
<commentary>
Since a logical chunk of code has been written (authentication feature), use the Task tool to launch the code-simplifier agent to improve the code's clarity and maintainability while preserving functionality.
</commentary>
assistant: "Now let me use the code-simplifier agent to refine this implementation for better clarity and maintainability"
</example>

<example>
Context: The assistant has just fixed a bug by adding several conditional checks.
user: "Fix the null pointer exception in the data processor"
assistant: "I've added the necessary null checks to prevent the exception:"
<function call omitted for brevity>
<commentary>
After modifying code to fix a bug, use the code-simplifier agent to ensure the fix follows best practices and maintains code quality.
</commentary>
assistant: "Let me refine this bug fix using the code-simplifier agent to ensure it follows our best practices"
</example>

<example>
Context: The assistant has just refactored a function to improve performance.
user: "Optimize the data sorting algorithm for better performance"
assistant: "I've optimized the sorting algorithm. Here's the updated implementation:"
<function call omitted for brevity>
<commentary>
After completing a performance optimization task, use the code-simplifier agent to ensure the optimized code is also clear and maintainable.
</commentary>
assistant: "Now I'll use the code-simplifier agent to ensure the optimized code is also clear and follows our coding standards"
</example> (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:comment-analyzer: Use this agent when you need to analyze code comments for accuracy, completeness, and long-term maintainability. This includes (1) after generating large documentation comments or docstrings, (2) before finalizing a pull request that adds or modifies comments, (3) when reviewing existing comments for potential technical debt or comment rot, and (4) when you need to verify that comments accurately reflect the code they describe. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:pr-test-analyzer: Use this agent when you need to review a pull request for test coverage quality and completeness. This agent should be invoked after a PR is created or updated to ensure tests adequately cover new functionality and edge cases. Typical triggers include the user asking whether tests on a freshly-created PR are thorough, an updated PR adding new logic that needs coverage analysis, and a final pre-merge double-check before marking a PR ready. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:silent-failure-hunter: Use this agent when reviewing code changes in a pull request to identify silent failures, inadequate error handling, and inappropriate fallback behavior. This agent should be invoked proactively after completing a logical chunk of work that involves error handling, catch blocks, fallback logic, or any code that could potentially suppress errors. Examples:

<example>
Context: Daisy has just finished implementing a new feature that fetches data from an API with fallback behavior.
Daisy: "I've added error handling to the API client. Can you review it?"
Assistant: "Let me use the silent-failure-hunter agent to thoroughly examine the error handling in your changes."
<Task tool invocation to launch silent-failure-hunter agent>
</example>

<example>
Context: Daisy has created a PR with changes that include try-catch blocks.
Daisy: "Please review PR #1234"
Assistant: "I'll use the silent-failure-hunter agent to check for any silent failures or inadequate error handling in this PR."
<Task tool invocation to launch silent-failure-hunter agent>
</example>

<example>
Context: Daisy has just refactored error handling code.
Daisy: "I've updated the error handling in the authentication module"
Assistant: "Let me proactively use the silent-failure-hunter agent to ensure the error handling changes don't introduce silent failures."
<Task tool invocation to launch silent-failure-hunter agent>
</example> (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:type-design-analyzer: Use this agent when you need expert analysis of type design in your codebase. Specifically use it (1) when introducing a new type to ensure it follows best practices for encapsulation and invariant expression, (2) during pull request creation to review all types being added, and (3) when refactoring existing types to improve their design quality. The agent will provide both qualitative feedback and quantitative ratings on encapsulation, invariant expression, usefulness, and enforcement. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

content.content:
---HOME CONFIG---
total 12
drwxrwxr-x 1 janfr janfr   78 jul 18 12:19 .
drwx------ 1 janfr janfr 1832 jul 17 16:52 ..
-rw-rw-r-- 1 janfr janfr 7567 jul 18 11:57 config.yaml
-rw-rw-r-- 1 janfr janfr  170 nov 29  2025 config.yaml.bak-stale-schema
# PC-switcher production configuration — this machine (nvme0n1p5, btrfs @home)
# Location: ~/.config/pc-switcher/config.yaml
#
# Syncs a single folder: /home/janfr. rsync mirror with --delete, so the target's
# /home/janfr is made identical to this machine's EXCEPT the excludes below.
#
# Exclude philosophy for THIS setup: the point of syncing is to continue work on
# the travel laptop offline (e.g. on a train) without re-downloading tens of GB.
# So big-but-useful regenerable data is KEPT: dev/ in full (node_modules, .venv,
# target/, .git), the uv/npm/pip package caches, and ~/.local/share/uv (the Python
# interpreters those venvs point at). All synced machines are assumed amd64 +
# Ubuntu 24.04 with the same username (janfr), so venvs, native modules, and
# compiled extensions transfer working. Only the following are excluded.

logging:
  file: DEBUG      # keep full detail in log files
  tui: INFO        # concise terminal output during sync
  external: WARNING

sync_jobs:
  dummy_success: false   # test jobs off in production
  dummy_fail: false
  folder_sync: true

disk_space_monitor:
  preflight_minimum: "20%"
  runtime_minimum: "15%"
  warning_threshold: "25%"
  check_interval: 30

btrfs_snapshots:
  # Only /home (subvol @home) is modified by this sync, so that is the one
  # subvolume worth snapshotting for rollback safety. Add "@" if you later
  # sync root-owned paths too.
  subvolumes:
    - "@home"
  keep_recent: 3

folder_sync:
  folders:
    - path: /home/janfr
      enabled: true
      # Excludes are rsync filter patterns relative to /home/janfr. A leading /
      # anchors to the top level of the home dir. pc-switcher's own runtime files
      # (~/.local/share/pc-switcher, its uv-tool install, the ~/.local/bin shim)
      # are excluded automatically and need not be listed (ADR-016).
      excludes:

        # --- Secrets: kept machine-specific ---
        # SSH private keys are per-machine auth identity — never copied between machines.
        - /.ssh/id_*
        # NOTE: .ssh/known_hosts IS synced — it holds only PUBLIC host keys of servers
        # you connect to (not a secret), and having the same trusted hosts on both
        # laptops is convenient. Only caveat: if the two laptops ever SSH into each
        # other, each will re-prompt once for the other's (differing) host key.

        # GNOME login keyring is encrypted with your login password/TPM; the blob
        # would not decrypt on the other machine, so it is useless there.
        - /.local/share/keyrings

        # NOTE: .gnupg and .password-store ARE synced — your GPG identity and the pass
        # store (itself GPG-encrypted) are user identity you want on both machines;
        # pass is useless without its GPG key, so they travel together.

        # --- Machine/hardware-specific: wrong or useless on different hardware ---
        - /.cache/nvidia                 # GPU shader cache — tied to this GPU model
        - /.cache/mesa_shader_cache
        - /.cache/mesa_shader_cache_db
        - /.nv                           # NVIDIA compute cache
        - /.cache/fontconfig             # font-path cache — rebuilt per machine
        - /.config/monitors.xml          # GNOME display layout — this machine's monitors
        - /.config/monitors.xml~
        - /.local/share/kscreen          # KDE screen/output config — this machine's displays

        # --- Desktop indexes & thumbnails: machine-local DBs, rebuilt automatically ---
        - /.local/share/baloo            # KDE file index (~838M) — rebuilt on target
        - /.cache/tracker3               # GNOME file index
        - /.cache/thumbnails
        - /.cache/gnome-software         # software-center metadata cache
        - /.cache/*.kcache               # KDE Plasma theme/icon caches

        # --- App runtime caches: churn constantly, no offline-work value ---
        # (Dev tooling caches like .cache/uv, .cache/pip, .npm are deliberately NOT here.)
        - /.cache/google-chrome
        - /.cache/spotify
        - /.cache/electron
        - /.cache/IBKR Desktop
        - /.cache/falco-app-updater
        - /.cache/plasmashell
        - /.config/Code/Cache
        - /.config/Code/CachedData
        - /.config/Code/GPUCache
        - /.config/Code/Code Cache
        - /.config/Code/CachedExtensionVSIXs
        - /.config/Claude/Cache
        - /.config/Claude/Code Cache
        - /.config/Claude/GPUCache
        - /.config/Claude/DawnWebGPUCache
        - /.config/Claude/DawnGraphiteCache
        - /.var/app/*/cache              # flatpak per-app caches (Firefox ~1.3G, etc.)

        # --- Large machine-local regenerable, no value on the other machine ---
        # Claude Desktop's Cowork / local-agent VM (~30G; the .cowork-adopted marker
        # names it). rootfs.img is a materialization of a versioned base (re-hydrated
        # from rootfs.img.zst or re-downloaded); sessiondata.img is the only truly
        # local state, and only matters if you run Cowork sessions. Not using Cowork,
        # so nothing is lost and no download results from excluding it — Desktop only
        # fetches the VM when you actually start a local-agent session. If you adopt
        # Cowork and want it offline, pre-sync this over LAN with Claude Desktop CLOSED
        # (VM stopped => consistent copy; only mid-session syncing risks a torn image).
        - /.config/Claude/vm_bundles
        - /.local/share/Trash            # trash can

        # --- Flatpak app binaries: OSTree content store ---
        # Must be provisioned with `flatpak install`, not rsynced (hardlinked,
        # content-addressed; rsync can corrupt/desync it). App DATA under .var/app
        # IS synced, so once the app is installed on the target its settings/state
        # are already there. (A future pc-switcher packages job will install these.)
        - /.local/share/flatpak

        # --- Device-linked app: won't work when copied ---
        - /.var/app/org.signal.Signal    # Signal Desktop is linked per-device; copying its data breaks the link

        # --- Cloud-synced folders: each machine runs its own daemon ---
        # Only the machine-STATE and the CONTENT are excluded, NOT the client binaries.
        # Copying the daemon STATE would make Dropbox/Koofr think the travel laptop
        # is this same install (shared host id / file DB) -> unlink, re-index, or
        # conflicts. Copying the CONTENT folders on top of an independently-running
        # daemon causes double-sync and "conflicted copy" files. So each machine
        # links its own daemon (log in once on the target) and the content is left
        # to those daemons entirely.
        # NOTE: .dropbox-dist and .koofr-dist (the client binaries) ARE synced.
        # Koofr has no package-manager install — ~/.koofr-dist IS the whole app
        # (autostart runs /home/janfr/.koofr-dist/storagegui), so excluding it would
        # leave Koofr missing on the target. Dropbox's daemon self-re-downloads, but
        # syncing it (same arch) avoids a ~150M fetch on the road.
        - /.dropbox                      # daemon state (host id, file DB) — per machine
        - /Dropbox                       # content — handled by the Dropbox daemon
        - /.koofr                        # daemon state + config.json (per-machine link/selective-sync)
        - /Koofr                         # content — handled by the Koofr daemon

        # --- Session runtime junk ---
        - /.xsession-errors              # X session log for this boot

  folders:
    - path: /home/janfr
      enabled: true
      # Excludes are rsync filter patterns relative to /home/janfr. A leading /
      # anchors to the top level of the home dir. pc-switcher's own runtime files
      # (~/.local/share/pc-switcher, its uv-tool install, the ~/.local/bin shim)
      # are excluded automatically and need not be listed (ADR-016).
      excludes:

        # --- Secrets: kept machine-specific ---
        # SSH private keys are per-machine auth identity — never copied between machines.
        - /.ssh/id_*
        # NOTE: .ssh/known_hosts IS synced — it holds only PUBLIC host keys of servers
        # you connect to (not a secret), and having the same trusted hosts on both
        # laptops is convenient. Only caveat: if the two laptops ever SSH into each
        # other, each will re-prompt once for the other's (differing) host key.

        # GNOME login keyring is encrypted with your login password/TPM; the blob
        # would not decrypt on the other machine, so it is useless there.
        - /.local/share/keyrings

        # NOTE: .gnupg and .password-store ARE synced — your GPG identity and the pass
        # store (itself GPG-encrypted) are user identity you want on both machines;
        # pass is useless without its GPG key, so they travel together.

        # --- Machine/hardware-specific: wrong or useless on different hardware ---
        - /.cache/nvidia                 # GPU shader cache — tied to this GPU model
        - /.cache/mesa_shader_cache
        - /.cache/mesa_shader_cache_db
        - /.nv                           # NVIDIA compute cache
        - /.cache/fontconfig             # font-path cache — rebuilt per machine
        - /.config/monitors.xml          # GNOME display layout — this machine's monitors
        - /.config/monitors.xml~
        - /.local/share/kscreen          # KDE screen/output config — this machine's displays

        # --- Desktop indexes & thumbnails: machine-local DBs, rebuilt automatically ---
        - /.local/share/baloo            # KDE file index (~838M) — rebuilt on target
        - /.cache/tracker3               # GNOME file index
        - /.cache/thumbnails
        - /.cache/gnome-software         # software-center metadata cache
        - /.cache/*.kcache               # KDE Plasma theme/icon caches

        # --- App runtime caches: churn constantly, no offline-work value ---
        # (Dev tooling caches like .cache/uv, .cache/pip, .npm are deliberately NOT here.)
        - /.cache/google-chrome
        - /.cache/spotify
        - /.cache/electron
        - /.cache/IBKR Desktop
        - /.cache/falco-app-updater
        - /.cache/plasmashell
        - /.config/Code/Cache
        - /.config/Code/CachedData
        - /.config/Code/GPUCache
        - /.config/Code/Code Cache
        - /.config/Code/CachedExtensionVSIXs
        - /.config/Claude/Cache
        - /.config/Claude/Code Cache
        - /.config/Claude/GPUCache
        - /.config/Claude/DawnWebGPUCache
        - /.config/Claude/DawnGraphiteCache
        - /.var/app/*/cache              # flatpak per-app caches (Firefox ~1.3G, etc.)

        # --- Large machine-local regenerable, no value on the other machine ---
        # Claude Desktop's Cowork / local-agent VM (~30G; the .cowork-adopted marker
        # names it). rootfs.img is a materialization of a versioned base (re-hydrated
        # from rootfs.img.zst or re-downloaded); sessiondata.img is the only truly
        # local state, and only matters if you run Cowork sessions. Not using Cowork,
        # so nothing is lost and no download results from excluding it — Desktop only
        # fetches the VM when you actually start a local-agent session. If you adopt
        # Cowork and want it offline, pre-sync this over LAN with Claude Desktop CLOSED
        # (VM stopped => consistent copy; only mid-session syncing risks a torn image).
        - /.config/Claude/vm_bundles
        - /.local/share/Trash            # trash can

        # --- Flatpak app binaries: OSTree content store ---
        # Must be provisioned with `flatpak install`, not rsynced (hardlinked,
        # content-addressed; rsync can corrupt/desync it). App DATA under .var/app
        # IS synced, so once the app is installed on the target its settings/state
        # are already there. (A future pc-switcher packages job will install these.)
        - /.local/share/flatpak

        # --- Device-linked app: won't work when copied ---
        - /.var/app/org.signal.Signal    # Signal Desktop is linked per-device; copying its data breaks the link

        # --- Cloud-synced folders: each machine runs its own daemon ---
        # Only the machine-STATE and the CONTENT are excluded, NOT the client binaries.
        # Copying the daemon STATE would make Dropbox/Koofr think the travel laptop
        # is this same install (shared host id / file DB) -> unlink, re-index, or
        # conflicts. Copying the CONTENT folders on top of an independently-running
        # daemon causes double-sync and "conflicted copy" files. So each machine
        # links its own daemon (log in once on the target) and the content is left
        # to those daemons entirely.
        # NOTE: .dropbox-dist and .koofr-dist (the client binaries) ARE synced.
        # Koofr has no package-manager install — ~/.koofr-dist IS the whole app
        # (autostart runs /home/janfr/.koofr-dist/storagegui), so excluding it would
        # leave Koofr missing on the target. Dropbox's daemon self-re-downloads, but
        # syncing it (same arch) avoids a ~150M fetch on the road.
        - /.dropbox                      # daemon state (host id, file DB) — per machine
        - /Dropbox                       # content — handled by the Dropbox daemon
        - /.koofr                        # daemon state + config.json (per-machine link/selective-sync)
        - /Koofr                         # content — handled by the Koofr daemon

        # --- Session runtime junk ---
        - /.xsession-errors              # X session log for this boot

1	"""Generic folder sync job via rsync-over-SSH.
2	
3	Syncs configured folders from source to target, running rsync as root on both
4	ends (local `sudo -E rsync` on source, `--rsync-path='sudo rsync'` on target)
5	to preserve cross-owner file metadata including POSIX ACLs and xattrs.
6	
7	Safety relies on btrfs pre/post snapshots, the rsync --dry-run deletion log,
8	and the orchestrator topology check (ADR-015).  This module is a pure rsync
9	mirror: validate() checks system prerequisites only; execute() runs the
10	rsync transfer.
11	"""
12	
13	from __future__ import annotations
14	
15	import getpass
16	import re
17	import shlex
18	from collections.abc import AsyncIterator
19	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
20	from pathlib import Path
21	from typing import Any, ClassVar, override
22	
23	from pcswitcher.jobs.base import SyncJob
24	from pcswitcher.models import FirstSyncScope, Host, LogLevel, ProgressUpdate, ValidationError
25	
26	# Matches rsync --info=progress2 output, e.g.:
27	#   "9.53G 21% 317.26MB/s 0:00:28 (xfr#83063, to-chk=443926/538653)"
28	# Group 1: size token (e.g. "9.53G") — used to compute bytes_transferred (WR-01).
29	# Group 2: percent complete.  Group 3: files transferred so far.  Group 4: total-to-check.
30	_PROGRESS2_RE = re.compile(r"(\d+[\d.]*[KMGT]?)\s+(\d+)%\s+\S+\s+\S+\s+\(xfr#(\d+),\s*to-chk=\d+/(\d+)\)")
31	
32	# pc-switcher's own runtime state and installation live under the invoking
33	# user's home directory. Mirroring them between machines is unsafe:
34	# sync-history.json is the topology-safety state (ADR-015) and a --delete mirror
35	# would clobber the target's copy mid-sync; the lock file, logs, and the running
36	# install must stay machine-local. These paths are ALWAYS excluded from folder
37	# sync regardless of user config, and are the ONLY hardcoded excludes — every
38	# other exclusion is user-configurable (ADR-016).
39	_RUNTIME_EXCLUDE_RELPATHS: tuple[str, ...] = (
40	    ".local/share/pc-switcher",  # lock, sync-history.json, logs/
41	    ".local/share/uv/tools/pcswitcher",  # uv tool install (virtualenv)
42	    ".local/bin/pc-switcher",  # entry-point shim
43	)
44	
45	
46	@dataclass
47	class FolderEntry:
48	    """A single folder to sync with its include/exclude configuration.
49	
50	    Excludes map to rsync `--filter=- <pattern>` arguments in order (first-match-wins).
51	    """
52	
53	    path: str
54	    enabled: bool = True
55	    excludes: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
56	
57	    def to_rsync_filter_args(self) -> list[str]:
58	        """Convert excludes to rsync --filter arguments.
59	
60	        Returns a list of `--filter=- <pattern>` strings in config order.
61	        First-match-wins semantics are preserved because the order of the list
62	        is the order in which rsync evaluates the filter rules.
63	        """
64	        return [f"--filter=- {pattern}" for pattern in self.excludes]
65	
66	
67	class FolderSyncJob(SyncJob):
68	    """Generic folder sync via rsync-over-SSH.
69	
70	    Syncs configured folders from source to target, running rsync as root on
71	    both ends to preserve cross-owner file metadata.
72	
73	    Config shape (mirrors config-schema.yaml `folder_sync` section):
74	        folders:
75	          - path: /home
76	            enabled: true          # default
77	            excludes: [...]        # rsync filter patterns, optional
78	    """
79	
80	    name: ClassVar[str] = "folder_sync"
81	
82	    CONFIG_SCHEMA: ClassVar[dict[str, Any]] = {
83	        "type": "object",
84	        "properties": {
85	            "folders": {
86	                "type": "array",
87	                "items": {
88	                    "type": "object",
89	                    "properties": {
90	                        "path": {"type": "string"},
91	                        "enabled": {"type": "boolean", "default": True},
92	                        "excludes": {
93	                            "type": "array",
94	                            "items": {"type": "string"},
95	                            "default": [],
96	                        },
97	                    },
98	                    "required": ["path"],
99	                    "additionalProperties": False,
100	                },
101	                "minItems": 1,
102	            }
103	        },
104	        "required": ["folders"],
105	        "additionalProperties": False,
106	    }
107	
108	    @staticmethod
109	    def _parse_size_to_bytes(value: str) -> int:
110	        """Convert an rsync progress figure (e.g. '9.53G', '317K', '80.153.795.479') to bytes.
111	
112	        Two shapes occur in --info=progress2 output:
113	        - the plain byte counter, which rsync groups with the locale thousands
114	          separator ('.' under nl_BE, ',' under en_US), e.g. '80.153.795.479';
115	        - a human-readable figure with a K/M/G/T suffix (only when rsync runs
116	          with -h), e.g. '9.53G', where the '.' is a decimal point.
117	
118	        rsync is forced to the C locale in `_build_rsync_cmd` so the counter is
119	        ungrouped in practice, but this parser stays tolerant of grouping so a
120	        stray locale-formatted figure can never abort the sync: the byte count
121	        is best-effort progress metadata (WR-01), not sync-critical.
122	
123	        Multipliers: K=1024, M=1024**2, G=1024**3, T=1024**4.
124	        """
125	        multipliers: dict[str, int] = {"K": 1024, "M": 1024**2, "G": 1024**3, "T": 1024**4}
126	        if value and value[-1].upper() in multipliers:
127	            # Suffixed figure carries a single decimal point; strip any comma grouping.
128	            return int(float(value[:-1].replace(",", "")) * multipliers[value[-1].upper()])
129	        # Plain integer counter: every '.'/',' is a thousands separator.
130	        return int(value.replace(".", "").replace(",", "") or "0")
131	
132	    def _active_folders(self) -> list[FolderEntry]:
133	        """Return only enabled folder entries from config."""
134	        return [
135	            FolderEntry(
136	                path=f["path"],
137	                enabled=f.get("enabled", True),
138	                excludes=f.get("excludes", []),
139	            )
140	            for f in self.context.config["folders"]
141	            if f.get("enabled", True)
142	        ]
143	
144	    @classmethod
145	    @override
146	    def describe_first_sync_scope(cls, config: dict[str, Any]) -> FirstSyncScope | None:
147	        """Describe the enabled folder paths this job would overwrite on a first sync.
148	
149	        Reproduces the same enabled-folder filter as `_active_folders`, but operates
150	        on a raw config dict (this is a classmethod, called before job instances
151	        exist) rather than `self.context.config`: an entry is in scope when it is a
152	        dict, its `enabled` (default True) is truthy, and its `path` is a str.
153	        """
154	        folders = config.get("folders", [])
155	        paths = [
156	            f["path"]
157	            for f in folders
158	            if isinstance(f, dict) and f.get("enabled", True) and isinstance(f.get("path"), str)
159	        ]
160	        if not paths:
161	            return None
162	        return FirstSyncScope(
163	            job_name=cls.name,
164	            scope_items=paths,
165	            mechanism="rsync --delete",
166	        )
167	
168	    async def validate(self) -> list[ValidationError]:
169	        """Validate system state before executing the folder sync.
170	
171	        Checks (in order):
172	        1. `sudo rsync --version` succeeds on source and target.
173	        2. `acl` package is installed on source and target (required for -A flag;
174	           without it rsync silently drops ACLs — RESEARCH Pitfall 5).
175	        3. Each active folder path exists on the source.
176	
177	        Returns the list of validation errors (empty on success).
178	        """
179	        errors: list[ValidationError] = []
180	
181	        # --- Step 1: sudo rsync availability ---
182	        result = await self.source.run_command("sudo rsync --version")
183	        if result.exit_code != 0:
184	            errors.append(
185	                self._validation_error(
186	                    Host.SOURCE,
187	                    "sudo rsync is not available on source (required for metadata-preserving transfer)",
188	                )
189	            )
190	
191	        result = await self.target.run_command("sudo rsync --version", login_shell=False)
192	        if result.exit_code != 0:
193	            errors.append(
194	                self._validation_error(
195	                    Host.TARGET,
196	                    "sudo rsync is not available on target (required for metadata-preserving transfer)",
197	                )
198	            )
199	
200	        # --- Step 2: acl package on both ends ---
201	        result = await self.source.run_command("dpkg -l acl | grep -q '^ii'")
202	        if result.exit_code != 0:
203	            errors.append(
204	                self._validation_error(
205	                    Host.SOURCE,
206	                    "acl package is not installed on source "
207	                    "(required for -A flag; without it rsync silently drops ACLs)",
208	                )
209	            )
210	
211	        result = await self.target.run_command("dpkg -l acl | grep -q '^ii'", login_shell=False)
212	        if result.exit_code != 0:
213	            errors.append(
214	                self._validation_error(
215	                    Host.TARGET,
216	                    "acl package is not installed on target "
217	                    "(required for -A flag; without it rsync silently drops ACLs)",
218	                )
219	            )
220	
221	        # --- Step 3: active folder existence on source ---
222	        for folder in self._active_folders():
223	            quoted = shlex.quote(folder.path)
224	            result = await self.source.run_command(f"test -d {quoted}")
225	            if result.exit_code != 0:
226	                errors.append(
227	                    self._validation_error(
228	                        Host.SOURCE,
229	                        f"folder {folder.path!r} does not exist on source",
230	                    )
231	                )
232	
233	        return errors
234	
235	    @staticmethod
236	    def _runtime_exclude_filters(folder_path: str) -> list[str]:
237	        """rsync `--filter` args protecting pc-switcher's own runtime files.
238	
239	        The runtime files (`_RUNTIME_EXCLUDE_RELPATHS`) live under the invoking
240	        user's home directory. When `folder_path` contains that home (e.g. syncing
241	        `/home`), each protected path is emitted as a root-anchored rsync pattern
242	        relative to the transfer root, so it matches exactly one location and can
243	        never be re-exposed by a user include rule. When home is outside
244	        `folder_path` (e.g. `/root` synced by a normal user) there is nothing to
245	        protect and the list is empty.
246	        """
247	        try:
248	            rel = Path.home().relative_to(folder_path.rstrip("/") or "/")
249	        except ValueError:
250	            return []
251	        prefix = "/" if str(rel) == "." else f"/{rel}/"
252	        return [f"--filter={shlex.quote(f'- {prefix}{sub}')}" for sub in _RUNTIME_EXCLUDE_RELPATHS]
253	
254	    def _build_rsync_cmd(self, folder: FolderEntry, dry_run: bool) -> str:
255	        """Build the rsync shell command for syncing a single folder.
256	
257	        Produces a command that:
258	        - Runs as root on source via `sudo -E rsync` (preserves SSH_AUTH_SOCK
259	          so the ssh agent socket remains accessible).
260	        - Elevates to root on target via `--rsync-path='sudo rsync'` over the
261	          normal-user SSH connection (D-05); root SSH login stays disabled.
262	        - Uses the D-13 flag baseline: -aAXHS + --numeric-ids + --delete.
263	        - Adds --dry-run only when `dry_run` is True (D-12).
264	        - Never includes --delete-excluded (excluded files must survive on target
265	          — D-06) or --checksum (rsync's built-in verification is trusted — D-14).
266	
267	        All config-derived values (folder path, exclude patterns, target hostname)
268	        are shlex.quote'd to prevent shell injection (T-05-01).
269	
270	        SSH transport note: `sudo -E rsync` runs rsync as root, and the ssh it
271	        spawns also runs as root.  OpenSSH resolves `~/.ssh` from the running
272	        uid's passwd entry (root → /root/.ssh) regardless of the $HOME variable.
273	        Passing explicit credentials (-l, -i, -o UserKnownHostsFile=, -F) with
274	        the invoking user's paths is the correct fix; relying on $HOME does NOT
275	        work (empirically verified on target VMs: ssh fails with "Host key
276	        verification failed" / rsync exit 255 without explicit credentials).
277	        """
278	        parts = [
279	            "sudo",
280	            "-E",
281	            # Force the C locale so rsync's --info=progress2 byte counter is
282	            # printed ungrouped.  Under a grouping locale (e.g. LC_NUMERIC=nl_BE)
283	            # rsync prints "80.153.795.479"; the progress2 regex captures the
284	            # whole token and float() then rejects the thousands separators,
285	            # aborting the sync over a purely cosmetic figure (WR-01).  `env`
286	            # runs after sudo so it sets the locale for rsync as root regardless
287	            # of sudo's own env policy.
288	            "env",
289	            "LC_ALL=C",
290	            "rsync",
291	            "-aAXHS",
292	            "--numeric-ids",
293	            "--delete",
294	            "--info=progress2",
295	            f"--out-format={shlex.quote('%i %n%L')}",
296	            "--partial",
297	            "--mkpath",
298	        ]
299	
300	        if dry_run:
301	            parts.append("--dry-run")
302	
303	        # Build explicit SSH credentials for the -e transport.
304	        # Background: sudo launches rsync as root; root's ssh resolves ~/.ssh
305	        # from /root/.ssh (uid lookup), not from $HOME.  Passing the invoking
306	        # user's assets explicitly is the verified fix (see docstring).
307	        home = Path.home()
308	        ssh_dir = home / ".ssh"
309	        target_user = self.context.target_username or getpass.getuser()
310	
311	        ssh_tokens: list[str] = ["ssh", "-T", "-q", "-l", target_user]
312	
313	        # -F before host-name flags so it takes effect for all connection params.
314	        config_file = ssh_dir / "config"
315	        if config_file.exists():
316	            ssh_tokens += ["-F", str(config_file)]
317	
318	        known_hosts_file = ssh_dir / "known_hosts"
319	        if known_hosts_file.exists():
320	            ssh_tokens += ["-o", f"UserKnownHostsFile={known_hosts_file}"]
321	
322	        # Offer all default key types that exist; ssh tries each in order.
323	        # SSH_AUTH_SOCK (preserved by sudo -E) is also available when an agent
324	        # is running, but explicit -i keys cover the no-agent case.
325	        for key_name in ("id_ed25519", "id_ecdsa", "id_rsa"):
326	            key_file = ssh_dir / key_name
327	            if key_file.exists():
328	                ssh_tokens += ["-i", str(key_file)]
329	
330	        # shlex.join quotes individual tokens (handles spaces in paths).
331	        # shlex.quote wraps the assembled command for the outer -e argument.
332	        ssh_cmd = shlex.join(ssh_tokens)
333	        parts.append(f"-e {shlex.quote(ssh_cmd)}")
334	
335	        # Root on target via passwordless sudo scoped to /usr/bin/rsync (D-05).
336	        parts.append(f"--rsync-path={shlex.quote('sudo rsync')}")
337	
338	        # Hardcoded protective excludes come FIRST (before user rules) so no user
339	        # include rule can re-expose pc-switcher's own runtime files (ADR-016).
340	        parts.extend(self._runtime_exclude_filters(folder.path))
341	
342	        # Per-folder filter rules in config order (first-match-wins for rsync).
343	        # DO NOT add --delete-excluded — excluded machine-specific files (e.g.
344	        # .ssh/id_*, .config/tailscale) must survive on the target (D-06).
345	        parts.extend(f"--filter={shlex.quote(f'- {pattern}')}" for pattern in folder.excludes)
346	
347	        # Source: trailing slash syncs contents, not the directory itself.
348	        src = shlex.quote(folder.path.rstrip("/") + "/")
349	        parts.append(src)
350	
351	        # Destination: <target_hostname>:<path>/ — rsync opens its own SSH
352	        # connection using ~/.ssh/config (ADR-002); the colon is rsync's own
353	        # remote-host separator, not a shell metacharacter.
354	        dst_raw = f"{self.context.target_hostname}:{folder.path.rstrip('/') + '/'}"
355	        parts.append(shlex.quote(dst_raw))
356	
357	        return " ".join(parts)
358	
359	    async def _stream_rsync(
360	        self,
361	        chunks: AsyncIterator[bytes],
362	        folder: FolderEntry,
363	    ) -> tuple[int, int, int]:
364	        """Stream rsync stdout, updating TUI progress and logging per-file events.
365	
366	        Handles `--info=progress2` output (carriage-return-delimited) and
367	        `--out-format` per-file lines (newline-delimited) in the same byte
368	        stream (RESEARCH Pitfall 2 — do not use readline-based iteration here).
369	
370	        Decoupled from the subprocess: consumes any `AsyncIterator[bytes]` so
371	        it can be unit-tested with a fake async generator.
372	
373	        Args:
374	            chunks: Async byte-chunk source (e.g. `proc.read_stdout_chunks()`).
375	            folder: The folder being synced (used for log context).
376	
377	        Returns:
378	            Tuple of (files_transferred, bytes_transferred, files_deleted).
379	            bytes_transferred is a best-effort count based on the last progress
380	            line (progress2 doesn't expose a precise cumulative byte count).
381	        """
382	        files_xfr = 0
383	        bytes_xfr = 0
384	        files_deleted = 0
385	        buf = b""
386
